claims.claim: The temperature rise of a 3.5 Ah NCM 18650-type cell increases as discharge current increases, with a maximal temperature rise of 14.8°C at 2C discharge rate in a chamber set to 20°C.
claims.quote: the temperature rise of the cell increases as the current increases, especially at the end of discharge. The temperature rises gently and slightly below the 1C discharge current rate but rapidly and drastically above 2C, for which the maximal temperature rise reaches 14.8C at 2C.

claims.claim: At discharge current rates below 1C, the temperature rise of the cell is sufficiently small (below 3°C when SOC is as low as 0.2 at 1C) that estimated parameters can be treated as true values for lookup table preparation.
claims.quote: the temperature rise of 1C is below 3C when the SOC is as low as 0.2; therefore, the estimated results of discharge current rate (<1C) at different temperatures can be considered the true values of the corresponding temperature and prepared for lookup tables.

claims.claim: Parameters εe,n, εe,p, and εe,s are insensitive to variation, as output voltage curves remain anastomotic even when the input parameter is decreased to 0.04, while Ds,n, Ds,p, εs,n, and εs,p are sensitive parameters whose slight changes greatly influence model output, especially at the end of discharge.
claims.quote: Even when the input parameter is sharply decreased to 0.04, the output curves are all anastomotic, which indicates that the three parameters are insensitive... the output curves vary greatly, especially at the end of discharge, which demonstrates that the four parameters are sensitive parameters and that slight changes in their values can greatly influence the model's fidelity.

claims.claim: In the negative electrode, both Ds,n and εs,n increase with increasing temperature, but only Ds,n increases appreciably with increasing current.
claims.quote: In the negative electrode, Ds,n (Figure 5a) and εs,n (Figure 5e) both increase with increasing temperature, but only Ds,n increases appreciably with increasing current.

claims.claim: The maximum estimated voltage error appears at the end of discharge (~0.25 SOC) due to strong nonlinear battery characteristics at extreme SOC, while voltage relative errors are within 0.5% for intermediate SOC across temperatures from 10°C to 40°C at 0.2C.
claims.quote: the maximum estimated error appears at the end of discharge (~0.25 SOC), generally because the battery exhibits strong nonlinear characteristics at extreme SOC universally... the voltage relative errors are essentially within 0.5%... the vehicle batteries mainly operate in the middle of the SOC, which indicates that the proposed method has high fidelity and strong robustness to temperature change.

claims.claim: The optimal operating temperature range of a Lithium-ion battery is 15°C to 35°C.
claims.quote: It is stated that the optimal temperature range of LiB is 15°C to 35°C

claims.claim: At low temperatures, the charge transfer resistance of a Lithium-ion battery is three times more than the resistance at room temperature.
claims.quote: In this situation, the charge transfer resistance will be three times more than the resistance at room temperature.

claims.claim: Heat generation in a Lithium-ion battery occurs in a sequential order: capacity loss and decomposition at 60°C, exothermic reaction affecting electrode material at 100°C, separator melting at 130°C, anode and cathode decomposition at 240°C, and at 800°C increased internal pressure leads to emission of harmful gas, smoke, fire, and explosion.
claims.quote: The heat generation is happening in the following order: (i) At 60°C - Capacity loss and decomposition, (ii) Exothermic reaction affects electrode material at 100°C, (iii) Separator melting at 130°C, (iv) Anode and cathode decomposition at 240°C, and (v) When the temperature reaches 800°C, it increases internal pressure and leads to emission of harmful gas, smokes, fire and unexpected explosion

claims.claim: Under the same pumping power, oil cooling provides 1.5 to 3 times better cooling response than water cooling.
claims.quote: it is understood that under the same pumping power (KW) oil cooling provides 1.5 to 3 times better response than water

claims.claim: During charging conditions, the maximum temperature was 37.5°C without cooling, 35.6°C with water cooling, and 34.7°C with oil cooling; during discharging conditions, the maximum temperature was 36.7°C without cooling, 35.3°C with water cooling, and 34.2°C with oil cooling.
claims.quote: During the charging conditions, the maximum temperature was maintained at 37.5°C, 35.6°C, and 34.7°C for no-cooling, air-cooling, and liquid-cooling, respectively. Similarly, the maximum temperature was maintained at 36.7°C, 35.3°C, and 34.2°C for no-cooling, air-cooling, and liquid-cooling, respectively, during discharging conditions.

claims.claim: When short-circuit resistance was 1 or 3 μΩ, the battery temperature rapidly exceeded 498 K at 21 s after discharge and voltage quickly decreased to near-zero within 40 s.
claims.quote: When the short circuit resistance was 1 and 3 μΩ, heat generated by the short-circuit resistance, when a large current was passed through rapidly, accumulated and could not be quickly dissipated to the surroundings. As a result, the temperature of the battery rapidly exceeded 498 K at 21 s after discharge and correspondingly the voltage quickly decreased to near-zero within 40 s

claims.claim: When short-circuit resistance was 30 μΩ, the local maximum temperature did not rise until a 70-s delay and reached only 430 K, after which temperature rapidly decreased because heat dissipation rate exceeded heat generation rate.
claims.quote: when the short-circuit resistance increased by one order of magnitude, for example at 30 μΩ as shown in Fig. 6, voltage showed a gentle decrease process for about 50 s and then began a rapid decrease lasting for 30 s. After 80 s, the voltage began to decrease slowly to zero. The concentrated release of energy was much slower than that in the two aforementioned conditions, so that Ohmic heat generated from short-circuit current had relatively enough time to be dissipated to the surroundings and environment, and therefore, the temperature of the local short-circuit location did not rise to the maximum (430 K) until a 70-s delay. After that, owing to the rate of heat generation being smaller than the rate of heat dissipation, the temperature rapidly decreased from the maximum.

claims.claim: Under ISC conditions with discharge rate 1C and ISC resistance 1 μΩ, the maximum temperature at the bottom-center location C was 413 K, at top-center location A was 411 K, and at center location B was 409 K within 5 s.
claims.quote: When the short circuit appeared at central location B, the temperature rose at the slowest rate to the maximum temperature of 409 K, followed by that at A (the maximum temperature being 411 K), while the temperature rise rate in the bottom location C was the fastest (the maximum temperature being 413 K).

claims.claim: Five seconds after ISC occurrence, the maximum temperature of the battery showed a linear decreasing trend with increasing discharge rate, and this decreasing trend diminished with increasing short-circuit resistance.
claims.quote: Five seconds after the occurrence of a short circuit, the maximum temperature of the battery showed a linear decreasing trend with an increasing discharge rate and such a trend tended to diminish with increasing short-circuit resistance.

claims.claim: Under ISC conditions with short-circuit resistance 0.5 μΩ, the electrochemical reaction heat generation accounted for about 80% of total heat generation, Ohmic heat from short-circuit current accounted for about 20%, and Ohmic volumetric heat generation was no more than 1% of the total.
claims.quote: the heat generation rate of the electrochemical reaction accounts for about 80%, and Ohmic heat generation from the short-circuit current accounts for about 20%, of the total heat generation, however, the proportion of Ohmic volumetric heat generation is no more than 1% overall.
